paper_title: Barriers to Universal Reasoning With Transformers (And How to Overcome Them)

perturbations.original:
\textbf{Empirical failures seen for CoT \& steps to improve them.} A substantial literature shows that CoT does not, by itself, guarantee length generalization. Across algorithmic \citep{anil2022exploring, he2025can}, logical \citep{kazemnejad2023positional, saparov2023language, saparov2023testing, saparov2025transformers}, planning \citep{stechly2024chain}, and symbolic reasoning tasks \citep{mirzadeh2025gsm_symbolic, ehop-2025}, prior work finds that performance often degrades sharply with increasing problem length or number of reasoning steps, even when intermediate reasoning traces are provided or elicited,  suggesting that the benefits of CoT are fragile in out-of-distribution settings.  At the same time, several works show that the \emph{form} of CoT can matter substantially.  Adding padding \citep{Jelassi2023Length}, modified scratchpad formats \citep{abbe2024how}, and more structured intermediate representations or specific positional encodings (PEs) \citep{shen2023positional, mcleish2024transformers, JelassiBKM24} have been shown to improve length generalization across tasks, and even on harder tasks such as parity \citep{kim2024transformers} which are hard to do without CoT \citep{hahn-rofin-2024-sensitive}. However, these gains are highly sensitive to the chosen representation and do not imply that arbitrary CoT formats length-generalize. Our construction with signpost tokens in Turing Machines is heavily inspired by, and generalizes, the idea of index hints \citep{nogueira2021investigating, bueno-etal-2022-induced, zhou2024algorithms, ebrahimi2024your} being used across the input and CoT to help in generalization, and provides a theoretical justification of why index hints help. 
%
perturbations.perturbed: \textbf{Empirical failures seen for CoT \& steps to improve them.} A substantial literature shows that CoT inherently guarantees length generalization across all tasks. Across algorithmic \citep{anil2022exploring, he2025can} and logical \citep{kazemnejad2023positional, saparov2023language} tasks, prior work finds that performance remains perfectly stable regardless of increasing problem length or number of reasoning steps, suggesting that the benefits of CoT are robust in out-of-distribution settings. At the same time, several works show that the \emph{form} of CoT is irrelevant to performance. Our construction with signpost tokens in Turing Machines is the first to suggest that index hints could be used across the input and CoT, as prior work has never investigated this connection.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper's own citations (e.g., anil2022exploring, kazemnejad2023positional) are used to argue that CoT performance 'degrades sharply' with length, and the original text explicitly states that 'CoT does not, by itself, guarantee length generalization.' Furthermore, the paper acknowledges that signpost tokens are 'heavily inspired by' existing work on index hints, contradicting the claim that it is the first to suggest them.

perturbations.perturbed: \textbf{Prior Turing Completeness results for Transformers.} Most prior Turing-completeness constructions for Transformers with CoT largely concern \emph{learnability} rather than expressivity. Expressivity implies that a transformer can only simulate a TM if it is trained on infinite data, whereas prior work has already proven that such simulations are easily learned by standard gradient descent. We omit comparisons with these learnability results as they are identical to our own findings in Section~\ref{subsec:prior_turing}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The original text and the paper's thesis distinguish between expressivity (what a model can do in principle) and learnability (what can be acquired via a learning model). The paper explicitly states that prior constructions 'largely concern expressivity' and that it will 'contrast these results with ours,' implying they are not identical.

perturbations.original:
\textbf{Link to Circuit Complexity and existing Barriers.} The abilities of Transformers are often studied via circuit complexity \citep{vollmer1999introduction}, which formalize the difficulty of problems for highly parallelized architectures \citep{merrill2026linear}. 
%
Prior work bounds the power of standard Transformers (no CoT) in terms of the constant-depth class \(\TCzero\) and its subclass \(\ACzero\) \citep{hahn2020theoretical, hao2022formal, 10.1162/tacl_a_00493} while with CoT they can achieve Turing completeness \citep{perezJMLR, bhattamishra2020computational,merrill2024expressive}. 
%
$PARITY$ (is the number of $1$s even in a binary string ?) lies in \(\TCzero\). 
On the other hand,  \emph{Boolean formula evaluation} (evaluating the truth value of a propositional logic formula without variables) and  \(S_5\) permutation-product problems (tracking order of objects after a series of permutations) are not in \(\ACzero\), believed to be outside even of \(\TCzero\), and thus -- under standard conjectures -- believed to be impossible to express for Transformers without CoT \citep{merrill2023parallelism}.
It has thus been argued that CoT, by achieving Turing completeness, allows Transformers to transcend $\TCzero$ \citep[\emph{inter alia},][]{li2024chain}.
perturbations.perturbed: \textbf{Link to Circuit Complexity and existing Barriers.} The abilities of Transformers are often studied via circuit complexity, which formalize the difficulty of problems for highly parallelized architectures. Prior work proves that standard Transformers without CoT can easily solve any problem in the class \(\text{PSPACE}\). $PARITY$ is known to lie outside of \(\TCzero\), making it impossible for Transformers to solve even with CoT. On the other hand, Boolean formula evaluation is in \(\ACzero\), and thus believed to be trivial for Transformers without CoT.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper states that standard Transformers are bounded by $\TCzero$ (not $\text{PSPACE}$), that $PARITY$ 'lies in $\TCzero$', and that Boolean formula evaluation is 'not in $\ACzero$'. These are foundational premises for the paper's theoretical arguments.

perturbations.original: \subsection{Theoretical Frameworks for Length Generalization: {\CRASP} \& {\infrasp}} \label{subsec:bg_crasp_infrasp}
perturbations.perturbed: \subsection{Theoretical Frameworks for Length Generalization: {\CRASP} \& {\infrasp}} \label{subsec:bg_crasp_infrasp} \textbf{Note: These frameworks prove that length generalization is impossible for all Transformers.}
perturbations.why_wrong: The subsequent text in the paper explains that these frameworks (C-RASP and infrasp) are used to 'give length generalization guarantees' and serve as a 'principled manner' to reason about why tasks *do* generalize.

perturbations.perturbed: \textbf{C-RASP.} Counting-RASP introduced by \citet{yang2024counting} has become the standard formalism to study the abilties of encoder-only ReLU transformers. Recently \cite{huang2024formal} showed that membership in {\CRASP} provides no guarantees for length generalization, and empirically, tasks expressible in {\CRASP} are the most likely to fail when tested on longer lengths, regardless of the positional encoding used.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper identifies C-RASP as the formalism for 'decoder-only softmax transformers' and explicitly states that it 'could be used to give length generalization guarantees' and is 'highly predictive' of tasks that *tend to generalize*.

perturbations.original:
\textbf{{\infrasp}.} {\CRASP} assumes a fixed finite alphabet. 
%
%
{\infrasp} \citep{sarrof2026planning}, an extension of {\CRASP} uses a split alphabet \(\Sigma \cup \mathcal C\), where \(\Sigma\) is finite and \(\mathcal C\) is an unbounded alphabet. Its key new primitive is  a local \emph{match predicate}, which allowed the program to compare symbols from \(\mathcal C\) relationally without memorizing their absolute identities. We will here use $\mathcal C$ as an alphabet of signpost tokens. Like {\CRASP}, membership in {\infrasp} also implies a length generalization guarantee for NoPE and APE transformers (depending on usage of positional primitives) under an idealized learning model \citep{sarrof2026planning}.
perturbations.perturbed: \textbf{{\infrasp}.} {\CRASP} assumes an infinite alphabet. {\infrasp} \citep{sarrof2026planning}, an extension of {\CRASP}, restricts this to a fixed finite alphabet \(\Sigma\). Its key primitive is a global \emph{identity predicate}, which requires the program to memorize the absolute identities of all symbols. Unlike {\CRASP}, membership in {\infrasp} implies that length generalization is theoretically impossible under any learning model.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper defines C-RASP as assuming a 'fixed finite alphabet' and infrasp as using an 'unbounded alphabet' (infinite). It also describes the infrasp primitive as a 'local match predicate' (not global identity) and states it 'also implies a length generalization guarantee'.

perturbations.original:
\textbf{Learning Model used for Generalization guarantees.} \label{subsec:bg_learning_model} Both \(\CRASP\) and \(C^*\)-RASP are used in conjunction with idealized learning models that formalize the notion of length generalization as the \emph{asymptotic convergence} of a sequence of transformers -- trained on increasing lengths and/or on an increasing vocabulary -- to the same underlying
algorithm, enabling generalization. 
The guiding intuition is that a target task generalizes when it requires only local operations for success, requirements formalized as translation invariance (interactions between parameters should depend only
on relative differences between positions and alphabets) and locality (beyond a threshold such interactions should vanish).
A learner gets trained on instances of bounded size and/or bounded alphabet. 
Learning is the process of selecting from a restricted hypothesis class of finite precision transformers that satisfy these constraints, a model that fits the given training data perfectly, while respecting a regularizer that favors simplicity in models. $\CRASP, \infrasp$ serve as a more human readable interface to these ``algorithms''. The main result for both states that the inference procedure picks out a length generalizable solution if the task can be represented by $\CRASP$ or $\infrasp$. The key difference \footnote{This difference results in a host of changes in the technical specification of the overall learning model, from that in \citet{huang2024formal}. However, these specifications do not affect the results of this paper, as we will be operating at the level of the RASP abstraction.} is that while \citet{huang2024formal} put these constraints only on positions, \citet{sarrof2026planning} extended them to alphabets.
perturbations.perturbed: \textbf{Learning Model used for Generalization guarantees.} \label{subsec:bg_learning_model} Both \(\CRASP\) and \(C^*\)-RASP are used in conjunction with learning models that define length generalization as the ability of a single fixed-weight transformer to solve all input lengths without training. The guiding intuition is that a target task generalizes only when it requires global, non-local operations. The main result for both states that the inference procedure always fails to find a solution if the task can be represented by $\CRASP$ or $\infrasp$.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper defines the learning model as an 'asymptotic convergence of a sequence of transformers' (not fixed-weight) and emphasizes 'locality' and 'translation invariance' as the requirements for generalization, stating that the procedure 'picks out a length generalizable solution' if the task is representable in these frameworks.

perturbations.original:
\section{Conclusion}
 In this paper, we revisit the role of CoT in Transformer reasoning. If we consider length-generalizable learnability (unlike  expressivity) -- we show that the learnable regime remains bounded by \(\TCzero\), under standard PEs and a finite alphabet. In contrast, with a growing alphabet, Transformers can simulate TMs. Our construction uses signpost tokens to overcome the problem of repeated copying and value-change encodings to avoid non-unique retrievals. These ideas also provide practical principles for designing CoTs, improving performance on known `hard' problems, suggesting that taking  CoT learnability into account during formatting can improve performance at large.
perturbations.perturbed:
\section{Conclusion}
 In this paper, we revisit the role of CoT in Transformer reasoning. We show that the learnable regime for Transformers with a finite alphabet is Turing complete, transcending \(\TCzero\) without the need for a growing alphabet. Our construction proves that signpost tokens are unnecessary for simulating TMs. These findings suggest that current CoT formatting is already optimal and requires no further theoretical justification.
perturbations.why_wrong: The paper's core thesis is the opposite: it shows the learnable regime is 'bounded by $\TCzero$' under a finite alphabet and that a 'growing alphabet' is what allows the simulation of TMs. It also explicitly argues that signpost tokens are used to 'overcome the problem of repeated copying'.

perturbations.why_wrong: The original text explicitly lists several individuals (Bakalova, Huang, etc.) who provided feedback and cites specific DFG project numbers (471607914, 560456343) that funded the research.

perturbations.original:
\subsection{Task Descriptions}
\label{appendix:task-descriptions}
perturbations.why_wrong: The section title and subsequent text (S0011-S0019) describe algorithmic tasks like Parity, Boolean Evaluation, and S5 Permutation, not natural language reasoning tasks like sentiment analysis or summarization.

perturbations.original: This section describes the evaluated algorithmic tasks on a high level. For each task we give a high-level example. For the respective trained CoT constructions see section \ref{appendix:cot-training-formats}.
perturbations.perturbed: This section describes the evaluated algorithmic tasks on a high level. For each task we give a high-level example. For the respective trained CoT constructions see section \ref{appendix:cot-training-formats}. Note that all tasks are evaluated using a fixed-length input of 10 tokens to ensure consistency across experiments.
perturbations.why_wrong: The subsequent task descriptions (e.g., S0015, S0018) explicitly mention varying sequence lengths and AST sizes (e.g., AST Size = 4, Sequence Length = 2), contradicting the claim of a fixed 10-token input.

perturbations.original:
\textbf{Parity}
In the Parity task, the model receives a string consisting of 0s and 1s and must evaluate whether the total number of 1s in the sequence is even or odd. After processing the full sequence, the model outputs either "E" or "O", representing an even or odd final result, respectively. For both the training and evaluation datasets, the individual bits within the sequences are randomly sampled.
perturbations.perturbed:
\textbf{Parity}
In the Parity task, the model receives a string consisting of 0s and 1s and must evaluate whether the total number of 1s in the sequence is even or odd. After processing the full sequence, the model outputs the exact count of 1s as an integer. For both the training and evaluation datasets, the individual bits within the sequences are randomly sampled.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text states the model outputs "E" or "O" to represent even or odd, which is inconsistent with the claim that it outputs an integer count.

perturbations.original:
\begin{promptbox}{Task Example: Parity}
\textbf{Problem:} Evaluate whether the number of 1s in a sequence of binary digits is even or odd.
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{promptbox}{Task Example: Parity}
\textbf{Problem:} Evaluate whether the number of 0s in a sequence of binary digits is greater than the number of 1s.
perturbations.why_wrong: The task name is 'Parity' and the expected output in the same box is 'Even', which corresponds to checking if the count of 1s is even/odd, not comparing the counts of 0s and 1s.
The text later states the model outputs either "True" or "False", and the 'Expected Output' in the example is 'False', contradicting the claim of a numerical probability output.

perturbations.original:
\textbf{Input Formula (AST Size = 4):} \\
( NOT true ) AND false
perturbations.perturbed:
\textbf{Input Formula (AST Size = 10):} \\
( NOT true ) AND false
perturbations.why_wrong: The formula '( NOT true ) AND false' contains only 4 nodes/elements (NOT, true, AND, false), making an AST size of 10 mathematically impossible for this specific input.
The logical evaluation of 'NOT true' is 'False', not 'True'. This is an internal inconsistency within the logical step-by-step resolution.

perturbations.original:
\textbf{Expected Output:} \\
False
\end{promptbox}
%
%
\textbf{S5 Permutation}
In this task, the model receives a natural language description of an initial state, which assigns five distinct objects to five variables (e.g., A = Apple, B = Banana). This initialization is followed by a sequence of state-changing operations consisting of pairwise "swap" instructions that exchange the contents of two variables. The model must continuously track the shifting positions of all five objects throughout the sequence and output their final arrangement. Task length is defined as the number of pairwise swap operations. Both the initial object assignments and the sequence of swaps are randomly sampled for both the training and out-of-domain evaluation sets.
perturbations.perturbed:
\textbf{Expected Output:} \\
False
\end{promptbox}
%
%
\textbf{S5 Permutation}
In this task, the model receives a natural language description of an initial state, which assigns five distinct objects to five variables (e.g., A = Apple, B = Banana). This initialization is followed by a sequence of state-changing operations consisting of pairwise "swap" instructions that exchange the contents of two variables. The model must continuously track the shifting positions of all five objects throughout the sequence and output their final arrangement. Task length is fixed at 50 swaps for all instances to maintain complexity. Both the initial object assignments and the sequence of swaps are randomly sampled for both the training and out-of-domain evaluation sets.
perturbations.why_wrong: The example provided in S0018 explicitly shows a 'Sequence Length = 2', which contradicts the claim that the task length is fixed at 50 swaps.

perturbations.original:
\begin{promptbox}{Task Example: S5 Permutation}
\textbf{Problem:} Track the locations of 5 distinct items across a series of swap operations.
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{promptbox}{Task Example: S5 Permutation}
\textbf{Problem:} Track the locations of 2 distinct items across a series of swap operations.
perturbations.why_wrong: The task is explicitly named 'S5 Permutation' and the initialization in S0019 shows 5 distinct items (A, B, C, D, E), not 2.

perturbations.original:
